title: Wake-Sleep Library Learning for Operator Discovery
subtitle: Let Compression Find the Primitives — how DreamCoder/Lilo-style wake-sleep cycles can discover Schankian operators from data, deriving their number and nature rather than assuming them

children.children: This tutorial explores a potentially transformative approach: instead of hand-picking semantic primitives, let a compression-driven wake-sleep loop DISCOVER them. DreamCoder and Lilo show this works for program synthesis — we apply the same principle to Schankian operators. The number and nature of primitives emerge from Minimum Description Length pressure on event decomposition.

props.title: 1. The Problem: Who Decides What the Primitives Are?

children.children: Schank proposed ~14 primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, etc.) based on linguistic intuition. But why 14? Why not 7 or 40? And how do we know he picked the right ones? Every hand-crafted primitive set faces a legitimacy problem: the designer's biases shape what gets discovered.

props.analogy: Imagine you're organizing a workshop's tool collection. You COULD start with categories you think make sense (cutting tools, hammering tools, measuring tools). But a better approach: watch how workers actually use tools, notice which operations recur across different projects, and let the categories EMERGE from usage patterns. A 'cutting' primitive might split into 'precision-cutting' and 'rough-cutting' — or merge with 'shaping' — based on what the data demands. That's what wake-sleep learning does for semantic primitives.

children.children: Wake-sleep library learning offers a principled solution: start with no primitives, observe event decompositions, compress recurring patterns into reusable operators, and let Minimum Description Length (MDL) pressure determine how many and what kind of primitives emerge.
Three reasons this changes the game:
1. **No a priori commitment** — The primitive count is derived, not assumed. If the data says 11 primitives, great. If it says 23, that's a genuine discovery.
2. **Automatic granularity** — MDL pressure finds the sweet spot between too few operators (poor coverage) and too many (no compression). The optimal library size falls out naturally.
3. **Interpretability for free** — Lilo adds LLM auto-documentation: each discovered primitive gets a natural-language description. You don't just find operators — you understand what they mean.

DreamCoder (Ellis et al., 2021) is a program synthesis system that discovers reusable abstractions through a wake-sleep cycle. It was designed for programming domains (list manipulation, graphics, physics), but its architecture maps beautifully onto operator discovery.

DreamCoder's Three Phases:

🌅 WAKE — Solve tasks using current library of primitives
🌙 SLEEP-ABSTRACTION — Find recurring sub-programs, compress into new library entries
💤 SLEEP-DREAMING — Generate synthetic tasks to practice with new primitives

Repeat. Each cycle, the library grows more powerful and more compressed.
In the list manipulation domain, DreamCoder starts with basic operations (+, -, cons, car, cdr) and discovers higher-order functions:

Cycle 1: Solves [1,2,3] → [2,4,6] as: cons(*(car L) 2) (map-manually-rest...)
Cycle 2: Notices recurring 'apply-to-each' pattern → abstracts MAP
Cycle 3: Solves [1,2,3] → [2,4,6] as: (map (* 2) L)  ← 3x shorter!

Cycle 5: Discovers FILTER from recurring conditional patterns
Cycle 8: Discovers FOLD from recurring accumulation patterns

Library grows: {+,-,cons,car,cdr} → {+,-,cons,car,cdr,MAP,FILTER,FOLD,...}

The key insight: MAP, FILTER, and FOLD weren't programmed in — they were DISCOVERED because they compress the solution space. The same principle should find ATRANS, PTRANS, and MTRANS in event decomposition.

props.title: Wake-Sleep Cycle Simulator

children.children: Here's how the DreamCoder architecture maps onto Schankian operator discovery:

definitions.definition: Instead of programming puzzles, each 'task' is decomposing a natural language event sentence into operators over entities. 'The cat knocked the vase off the shelf' must be explained using current library operators.
A 'program' is an operator expression: PROPEL(cat, vase) ∘ PTRANS(vase, shelf→floor) ∘ STATE_Δ(vase, intact→broken). These compose operators from the current library.
The library IS the set of discovered primitives. It starts minimal and grows through abstraction. Each entry has a type signature: ATRANS : Agent × Object × Source × Goal → Event.
Minimize: |Library| × cost_per_primitive + Σ |decomposition(event_i)|. Too few primitives = expensive decompositions. Too many = expensive library. The optimum determines primitive count.

children.children: Minimum Description Length formalizes Occam's Razor. The total cost of a library L and dataset D is:

MDL(L, D) = |L| + Σᵢ |encode(dᵢ | L)|

where:
  |L| = cost to describe the library (each primitive's definition)
  |encode(dᵢ | L)| = cost to describe event dᵢ using library L

Optimal library L* = argmin_L MDL(L, D)

• Library {MOVE} alone: |L|=1, but encode('gave')=5 (need many MOVE compositions)
• Library {verb₁...verb₅₀₀₀}: |L|=5000, encode=1 (just look up the verb)
• Library {ATRANS,PTRANS,...}: |L|≈14, encode≈2-3 — THE SWEET SPOT

This is why ~14 primitives is not arbitrary — it's the compression optimum. Too few operators means you need long, complex decompositions for every event. Too many means the library itself is expensive to maintain. MDL finds the balance automatically.

props.title: MDL Cost Explorer

children.children: Lilo (Grand et al., 2023) extends DreamCoder with a crucial innovation: it uses LLMs to auto-document and name discovered abstractions, making the library interpretable. For operator discovery, this means each primitive gets a natural-language description explaining what it does.

props.analogy: Imagine an archaeologist who discovers unknown tools at a dig site. Pure DreamCoder is like cataloguing them as 'Tool #1, Tool #2' — you know they're distinct and reusable, but not what they're for. Lilo adds a cultural anthropologist who examines each tool's usage patterns and says 'This is a grain-grinding stone' or 'This is a hide-scraper.' The LLM provides the semantic grounding that pure compression lacks.

children.children: After the sleep-abstraction phase discovers a new operator, Lilo sends its usage examples to an LLM:

Discovered Operator λ₇:
  Used in: 'gave a gift', 'sold the car', 'donated blood', 'lent money', 'transferred funds'
  Type: Agent × Object × Recipient → Event
  Always co-occurs with: possession-change, volitional-agent

LLM Documentation:
  Name: ATRANS (Abstract Transfer)
  Description: "Volitional transfer of possession or control of an object
  from one agent to another, without requiring physical movement."
  Distinguishing feature: "Unlike PTRANS, the object need not physically
  move — ownership/control changes abstractly."
The LLM doesn't invent the primitive — it NAMES and EXPLAINS what compression already found. This bridges the gap between formal discovery and human understanding.

props.title: Lilo's stitch-based abstraction vs. DreamCoder's anti-unification

children.children: DreamCoder uses anti-unification to find common sub-expressions: it literally aligns two programs and extracts the shared structure, leaving differences as variables. This is expensive (exponential in the worst case).

Lilo replaces this with 'stitch' — a faster corpus-level compression algorithm that finds the single abstraction yielding the greatest total compression across ALL programs simultaneously. This scales to larger libraries and longer programs.

For operator discovery, stitch means we can process thousands of event decompositions at once, finding the operator abstractions that compress the ENTIRE event corpus most efficiently — not just pairwise similarities.

props.title: 5. The Three Sleep Phases for Operators

children.children: The full wake-sleep cycle for operator discovery has three distinct phases, each serving a critical role:

props.title: Wake-Sleep Phase Explorer

children.children: Critical design choice: should STEAL be a primitive, or a specialization of ATRANS? MDL decides. If STEAL occurs often enough to justify its library cost, it earns its place. If not, it stays as ATRANS(covert=true). The system doesn't need human judgment — compression pressure does the work.

props.title: 6. What Would the Discovered Library Look Like?

children.children: The exciting prediction: wake-sleep learning on a large event corpus should converge to a library that LOOKS LIKE Schank's primitives — even though it was never told about them. Here's what we might expect:

After ~10 wake-sleep cycles on 50K event sentences:

Discovered Operators (with Lilo names):
  λ₁  → "Physical-Transfer"    ≈ PTRANS   (move, carry, send)
  λ₂  → "Possession-Transfer"  ≈ ATRANS   (give, sell, steal)
  λ₃  → "Info-Transfer"        ≈ MTRANS   (tell, show, teach)
  λ₄  → "Force-Application"    ≈ PROPEL   (push, kick, throw)
  λ₅  → "Mental-Construction"  ≈ MBUILD   (decide, realize, plan)
  λ₆  → "Sensory-Focus"        ≈ ATTEND   (look, listen, smell)
  λ₇  → "Intake"               ≈ INGEST   (eat, breathe, absorb)
  λ₈  → "State-Transition"     ≈ STATE_Δ  (melt, break, grow)
  λ₉  → "Vocal-Production"     ≈ SPEAK    (say, shout, whisper)
  λ₁₀ → "Grasping"             ≈ GRASP    (hold, grab, release)
  λ₁₁ → "Body-Motion"          ≈ MOVE     (walk, swim, jump)
  λ₁₂ → "Expulsion"            ≈ EXPEL    (spit, exhale, vomit)
  λ₁₃ → ???                     — NOVEL    (no Schank equivalent!)

Surprise primitives the data might demand but Schank didn't include.

The λ₁₃ case is the most interesting. Wake-sleep might discover operators Schank MISSED — primitives that linguistic intuition overlooked but compression demands. Possible candidates: an EMOTION-CHANGE operator, a SOCIAL-RELATION operator, or a CREATION operator.
Here's a concrete implementation plan for wake-sleep operator discovery:

Architecture:

1. EVENT PARSER: Takes natural language → structured (subject, verb, object, ...) using an LLM
2. DECOMPOSER: Maps structured events → operator expressions using current library
3. STITCH COMPRESSOR: Finds max-compression abstractions across decomposition corpus
4. DREAM GENERATOR: Samples operator expressions → generates synthetic events via LLM
5. RECOGNITION MODEL: Trained on dreamed data → amortizes decomposition search
6. LLM DOCUMENTER: Names and describes each discovered operator

Data: Start with VerbNet event frames (~8K entries) + ATOMIC if-then tuples (~880K)

props.title: Implementation Architecture Diagram

children.children: **Challenge 1: Bootstrapping.** You need SOME operators to start decomposing. Solution: begin with Dowty's minimal set {DO, CAUSE, BECOME, GO} or even just {CHANGE, TRANSFER, APPLY-FORCE}. The wake-sleep loop will refine and expand from there.

**Challenge 2: Search complexity.** Finding the best decomposition of an event into operators is combinatorially hard. Solution: the dreaming phase trains a recognition model (neural network) that amortizes this search — it learns to instantly propose likely decompositions.

**Challenge 3: Evaluation.** How do you know the discovered primitives are 'right'? Solution: compare against Schank's, Dowty's, and VerbNet's primitives. Measure compression ratio. Test compositional generalization on held-out events. Check cross-linguistic consistency.
Wake-sleep library learning is a unifying framework that connects several other ideas in the brainstorm:

definitions.definition: The direct inspiration. Entry 54 explored DreamCoder for operator discovery in general; this entry makes the mapping concrete and adds Lilo's LLM documentation layer.
MDL is the objective function that wake-sleep optimizes. Entry 45 proposed MDL as a criterion; wake-sleep provides the optimization algorithm.
Circuit extraction finds primitives ALREADY LEARNED by transformers. Wake-sleep TRAINS a system to discover them. Complementary: use circuit extraction to validate wake-sleep's outputs.
Linguistic anchors (Dowty's DO/CAUSE/BECOME/GO) could bootstrap the wake-sleep library, giving compression a head start.
The operator library IS an information bottleneck — it compresses event descriptions to their essential semantic content. Wake-sleep finds the bottleneck that maximizes compression.

questions.question: What is the core advantage of wake-sleep library learning over hand-crafted primitives?
options: It's faster to implement
It derives the number and nature of primitives from data via compression pressure
It uses larger neural networks
It requires less training data
questions.explanation: The key advantage is that MDL pressure determines HOW MANY primitives exist and WHAT they are — you don't need a linguist's intuition to decide. The primitive count is derived, not assumed.

questions.question: What does the Sleep-Abstraction phase do?
options: Generates synthetic training data
Decomposes events into operator expressions
Finds recurring sub-expressions and compresses them into new library entries
Trains the recognition model
questions.explanation: Sleep-Abstraction examines all decompositions from the wake phase, identifies recurring patterns (sub-expressions that appear across many events), and abstracts them into new library entries. This is where new primitives are born.

questions.question: What role does the LLM play in Lilo's extension of DreamCoder?
options: It replaces the compression algorithm entirely
It auto-documents and names discovered abstractions for interpretability
It generates the initial set of primitives
It evaluates the quality of decompositions
questions.explanation: Lilo uses LLMs to provide natural-language names and descriptions for each discovered operator. The compression algorithm finds the primitives; the LLM explains what they mean in human-understandable terms.

questions.question: Why might wake-sleep discover primitives that Schank DIDN'T include?
options: Because it has access to more data than Schank did
Because compression pressure may demand operators that linguistic intuition missed
Because it uses random initialization
Because it over-fits to the training corpus
questions.explanation: MDL pressure finds the optimal set regardless of human preconceptions. If a recurring event pattern doesn't fit any known primitive but compresses better as a new operator, wake-sleep will create it. Schank's introspection may have blind spots that data-driven compression doesn't share.

questions.question: What is the MDL trade-off that determines the optimal number of primitives?
options: Speed vs accuracy of decomposition
Library cost (more primitives = expensive) vs decomposition cost (fewer primitives = longer expressions)
Training time vs inference time
Model size vs dataset size
questions.explanation: MDL minimizes total description length: library size + sum of decomposition lengths. A tiny library means each event needs long, complex decompositions. A huge library means the library itself is expensive. The optimum (~14 primitives) balances these costs.
